annotations.label: Cellular automaton

anchor.snippet: A cellular automaton consists of a regular grid of cells

annotations.note: No cellular automaton definition found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neighborhood of a cell

anchor.snippet: a set of cells called its neighborhood is defined relative to the specified cell

annotations.note: No CA neighborhood concept formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Initial state / new generation

anchor.snippet: An initial state (time t = 0) is selected by assigning a state for each cell

annotations.note: No CA initial-state/generation concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram classes (overview)

anchor.snippet: The primary classifications of cellular automata, as outlined by Wolfram, are numbered one to four

annotations.note: Wolfram classification of CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversible CA (lead)

anchor.snippet: Special types of cellular automata are reversible , where only a single configuration leads directly to a subsequent one

annotations.note: Reversible CA not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Totalistic CA (lead)

anchor.snippet: totalistic , in which the future value of individual cells only depends on the total value of a group of neighboring cells

annotations.note: Totalistic CA not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: von Neumann and Moore neighborhoods

anchor.snippet: The two most common types of neighborhoods are the von Neumann neighborhood and the Moore neighborhood

annotations.note: No von Neumann or Moore CA neighborhood found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extended von Neumann neighborhood

anchor.snippet: Another common neighborhood type is the extended von Neumann neighborhood , which includes the two closest cells in each orthogonal direction

annotations.note: Extended von Neumann neighborhood not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Total number of automata formula

anchor.snippet: The general equation for the total number of automata possible is

annotations.note: Counting formula for CA rules not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Moore neighborhood count

anchor.snippet: Thus, in the two-dimensional system with a Moore neighborhood, the total number of automata possible would be

annotations.note: Specific Moore neighborhood CA count not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Configuration

anchor.snippet: the assignment of state values is called a configuration

annotations.note: CA configuration concept not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Periodic boundary conditions / toroidal arrangement

anchor.snippet: These cells are usually handled with periodic boundary conditions resulting in a toroidal arrangement

annotations.note: CA periodic boundary conditions not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 1D neighborhood example

anchor.snippet: in a 1-dimensional cellular automaton like the examples below, the neighborhood of a cell

annotations.note: 1D CA neighborhood example not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Curtis–Hedlund–Lyndon theorem

anchor.snippet: The most fundamental result is the characterization in the Curtis–Hedlund–Lyndon theorem

annotations.note: Curtis–Hedlund–Lyndon theorem not found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Smith: 1D CA computation universal

anchor.snippet: He proved that two-dimensional CA are computation universal, introduced 1-dimensional CA, and showed that they too are computation universal

annotations.note: Smith's universality result for 1D/2D CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Game of Life rules

anchor.snippet: its rules are as follows: Any live cell with fewer than two live neighbours dies

annotations.note: Conway's Game of Life rules not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Game of Life is Turing-universal

anchor.snippet: after much effort it has been shown that the Game of Life can emulate a universal Turing machine

annotations.note: Turing-universality of Game of Life not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule 110 universality

anchor.snippet: suggested that Rule 110 may be universal —a fact proved later by Wolfram's research assistant Matthew Cook

annotations.note: Rule 110 universality not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram Class 1

anchor.snippet: Class 1: Nearly all initial patterns evolve quickly into a stable, homogeneous state

annotations.note: Wolfram Class 1 not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram Class 2

anchor.snippet: Class 2: Nearly all initial patterns evolve quickly into stable or oscillating structures

annotations.note: Wolfram Class 2 not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram Class 3

anchor.snippet: Class 3: Nearly all initial patterns evolve in a pseudo-random or chaotic manner

annotations.note: Wolfram Class 3 not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram Class 4

anchor.snippet: Class 4: Nearly all initial patterns evolve into structures that interact in complex and interesting ways

annotations.note: Wolfram Class 4 not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Class 4 universality conjecture

anchor.snippet: Wolfram has conjectured that many class 4 cellular automata, if not all, are capable of universal computation

annotations.note: Wolfram's Class 4 universality conjecture not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Culik–Yu classes

anchor.snippet: Culik and Yu proposed three well-defined classes (and a fourth one for the automata not matching any of these)

annotations.note: Culik–Yu classification not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Undecidability of Culik–Yu membership

anchor.snippet: membership in these proved undecidable

annotations.note: Undecidability of Culik–Yu membership not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversible cellular automaton

anchor.snippet: A cellular automaton is reversible if, for every current configuration of the cellular automaton, there is exactly one past configuration

annotations.note: Reversible CA not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reversibility ⇔ bijective

anchor.snippet: reversibility implies that this function is bijective

annotations.note: CA-specific reversibility ↔ bijective transition not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Time-reversed reversible CA is a CA

anchor.snippet: If a cellular automaton is reversible, its time-reversed behavior can also be described as a cellular automaton

annotations.note: Time-reversed CA result not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Garden of Eden patterns

anchor.snippet: the configurations without preimages are called Garden of Eden patterns

annotations.note: Garden of Eden patterns not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Undecidability of reversibility in ≥2D

anchor.snippet: for cellular automata of two or more dimensions reversibility is undecidable

annotations.note: Undecidability of 2D CA reversibility not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Block CA emulates reversible CA

anchor.snippet: every reversible cellular automaton can be emulated by a block cellular automaton

annotations.note: Block CA emulation result not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Totalistic cellular automaton

anchor.snippet: The state of each cell in a totalistic cellular automaton is represented by a number

annotations.note: Totalistic CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Outer totalistic CA

anchor.snippet: If the state of the cell at time t depends on both its own state and the total of its neighbors at time t − 1 then the cellular automaton is properly called outer totalistic

annotations.note: Outer totalistic CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Life-like cellular automata

anchor.snippet: Outer totalistic cellular automata with the same Moore neighborhood structure as Life are sometimes called life-like cellular automata

annotations.note: Life-like CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Isotropic ruleset

anchor.snippet: an isotropic ruleset is one that is not necessarily outer totalistic, but still has all the reflection symmetries

annotations.note: Isotropic CA ruleset not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probabilistic cellular automata

anchor.snippet: Such cellular automata are called probabilistic cellular automata

annotations.note: Probabilistic CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous automata

anchor.snippet: There are continuous automata

annotations.note: Continuous CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Continuous spatial automata

anchor.snippet: Continuous spatial automata have a continuum of locations

annotations.note: Continuous spatial automata not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph rewriting automata

anchor.snippet: Graph rewriting automata are extensions of cellular automata based on graph rewriting systems

annotations.note: Graph rewriting automata not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elementary cellular automaton

anchor.snippet: The simplest nontrivial cellular automaton would be one-dimensional, with two possible states per cell

annotations.note: Elementary CA not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: 256 elementary rules

anchor.snippet: There are then 2 8 = 256 possible rules

annotations.note: Count of 256 elementary CA rules not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wolfram code

anchor.snippet: These 256 cellular automata are generally referred to by their Wolfram code

annotations.note: Wolfram code numbering not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule 30 class 3 behavior

anchor.snippet: Rule 30 exhibits class 3 behavior, meaning even simple input patterns such as that shown lead to chaotic, seemingly random histories

annotations.note: Rule 30 specific behavior not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rule 110 class 4 behavior

anchor.snippet: Rule 110, like the Game of Life, exhibits what Wolfram calls class 4 behavior

annotations.note: Rule 110 specific behavior not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: CA rule space

anchor.snippet: all elementary cellular automaton rules can be considered to sit on the vertices of the 8-dimensional unit hypercube

annotations.note: CA rule-space hypercube embedding not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hamming distance between rules

anchor.snippet: This rule-to-rule distance is also called the Hamming distance

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `hammingDist` for arbitrary indexed functions, generalizing the CA-rule-to-rule Hamming distance.

annotations.label: Class 4 between class 1 and 3

anchor.snippet: it is shown that class 4 rules are located between the class 1 and class 3 rules

annotations.note: Class 4 location result not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conus textile shell pattern

anchor.snippet: the widespread species Conus textile bears a pattern resembling Wolfram's rule 30 cellular automaton

annotations.note: Biology example not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ising model

anchor.snippet: The Ising model is a prototypical example, in which each cell can be in either of two states called "up" and "down"

annotations.note: Ising model not found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maze and Mazectric rules

anchor.snippet: Two well-known such cellular automata, Maze and Mazectric, have rulestrings B3/S12345 and B3/S1234

annotations.note: Maze/Mazectric CA rules not in Mathlib.
